B.text:
Permissions are gated in this session, so I'll give you the optimized code with the complexity analysis directly rather than running a live benchmark.

## Optimized version

```js
function dedup(a){return [...new Set(a)];}
```

## Why it's faster

| | Original | Optimized |
|---|---|---|
| Approach | `filter` + `indexOf` per element | single pass into a `Set` |
| Membership test | `indexOf` scans the array — O(n) each | `Set` add/has — O(1) average |
| **Total** | **O(n²)** | **O(n)** |

The original calls `a.indexOf(x)` for all `n` elements, and each `indexOf` is itself a linear scan → n × n work. A `Set` hashes each value, so the whole dedup is a single linear pass.

**Expected improvement:** roughly linear vs. quadratic. At n=10k you'd see ~hundreds× speedup; at n=100k it's the difference between seconds and ~1ms. The gap widens with n.

## Behavior — identical, with one nuance

- **Order preserved:** `Set` keeps first-insertion order, same as the original (which keeps the first occurrence via `indexOf(x) === i`).
- **`NaN`:** Actually *more correct*. The original drops `NaN` entirely because `indexOf(NaN)` always returns `-1` (never equals `i`). `Set` uses SameValueZero, so it correctly keeps a single `NaN`. If you specifically need the old buggy behavior, that's the one difference.
- **Object references:** both dedup by reference identity (unchanged).

If you can't use `Set` (very old environment), the equivalent O(n) fallback:

```js
function dedup(a){const seen=new Set();return a.filter(x=>!seen.has(x)&&seen.add(x));}
```

This avoids spreading and is handy when you want to keep `filter` semantics — same O(n), same `NaN`-correct behavior.

F.text:
```js
const dedup = a => [...new Set(a)];
```

`Set` insertion already enforces uniqueness, so building one and spreading it back to an array does the whole job in a single pass.

**Improvement:** O(n²) → O(n) time. The original calls `a.indexOf(x)` for every element, and each `indexOf` scans up to the full array — n elements × n-element scan = ~n² comparisons. The `Set` version inserts each element once with average O(1) hashing, so total work is linear. On a 10k-element array that's roughly 10,000 operations instead of ~100,000,000 — the gap widens quadratically as input grows.

Semantics are preserved: both keep the first occurrence of each value and maintain original order (`Set` iterates in insertion order).

One genuine behavior difference to be aware of: `NaN`. The original drops all `NaN`s, because `indexOf(NaN)` always returns `-1` and never matches `i`. The `Set` version keeps exactly one `NaN`, since `Set` treats `NaN` as equal to itself. The `Set` behavior is the more conventionally-correct dedup; if you specifically need the old NaN-dropping quirk, that's the one case to flag.
